title: Price Elasticity of Demand and its Determinants
description: Practice what you've learned about calculating and interpreting price elasticity of demand, as well as the determinants of price elasticity of demand, in this exercise.
topic_path: Price Elasticity of Demand and its Determinants

assessment_items.question_content:
**If an economist wants to measure the magnitude of a percentage change in quantity to changes in a good's price, what is the appropriate way to do that? **

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Economists would use a calculation, not a graph, to find the magnitude of a change in quantity in response to a change in price, income, or the prices of other goods.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes what *price elasticity of demand* measures?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: How responsive buyers are to a change in the price of a good
How aware producers are of a change in price
How much buyers change price in response to a change in quantity
How responsive sellers are to a change in the price of a good
How responsive buyers are to a change in income

assessment_items.question_content:
**Price elasticity of demand is a concept used to describe what concept in economics?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Elasticity is calculated as the percentage change in quantity demanded in response to a change in price.

choices.content: the magnitude of the change in quantity due to a change in price
the magnitude of the change in quantity due to a change in income
whether quantity demanded increases or decreases when price increases
how responsive output is to a change in government spending
how responsive sellers are to a change in the price of a good

assessment_items.question_content:
Gas prices rose by $12\%$ following a hurricane in the Gulf of Mexico. As a result, the amount of gas purchased in the week fell by $3\%$ following the price increase. 

**What is the price elasticity of demand for gas in the week following the price increase?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Calculate the price elasticity of demand using this formula:

$E_p^D =\Bigg| \dfrac{\% \text{change in quantity demanded}}{\% \text{change in price}} \Bigg|$

Remember that the convention in economics is to express price elasticity of demand as a positive number because we take the absolute value of it.

The price elasticity of demand is 0.25:

$\begin{align}E_p^D &=\Bigg| \dfrac{\% \text{change in quantity demanded}}{\% \text{change in price}} \Bigg|\\\\
&=\Bigg| \dfrac{-3\%}{12\%}\Bigg| \\\\
&=\Bigg| -0.25 \Bigg|\\\\
&=0.25\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
When the price of spicy sauce was $\$10$, people bought $100$ jars. When the price increased to $\$12$, people only bought $40$ jars.

**What is the price elasticity of demand for spicy sauce?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Calculate the price elasticity of demand using this formula:

$E_p^D =\Bigg| \dfrac{\% \text{change in quantity demanded}}{\% \text{change in price}} \Bigg|$
The percentage change in quantity demanded is the difference between the new quantity and the old quantity divided by the initial quantity.
The percentage change in price is the difference between the new price and the old price divided by the initial price.

The price elasticity of demand is 3:

$\begin{align}E_p^D &=\Bigg| \dfrac{\% \text{change in quantity demanded}}{\% \text{change in price}} \Bigg|\\\\
&=\Bigg|\dfrac{\dfrac{Q_2-Q_1}{Q_1} \times 100\%}{\dfrac{P_2-P_1}{P_1}\times 100\%}\Bigg|\\\\
&=\Bigg| \dfrac{\dfrac{40-100}{100}\times 100\%}{\dfrac{\$12 - \$10}{\$10}\times 100\%}\Bigg|\\\\
&=\Bigg|\dfrac{\dfrac{-60}{100}\times 100\%}{\dfrac{\$2}{\$10}\times 100\%} \Bigg|\\\\
&=\Bigg| \dfrac{60\%}{20\%}\Bigg|\\\\
&=\Bigg| -3\Bigg|\\\\
&=3\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
The table below contains the demand schedule for grapes.

 $P$ | $Q_D$ (pounds)
 - | -
$\$0.50$ | $1000$
$\$1.00$ | $800$
$\$1.50$ | $600$
$\$2.00$| $400$

**What is the price elasticity of demand for grapes when the price increases from \$0.50 to \$1.00?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The price elasticity of demand is the percentage change in quantity demanded in response to the percentage change in price:

$E_p^D =\Bigg| \dfrac{\% \text{change in quantity demanded}}{\% \text{change in price}} \Bigg|$ 

If we are only given prices and not percentage changes, we must first calculate the percentage changes. We can calculate the percentage change in quantity demanded and the percentage change in price using this method:

$\begin{align}E_p^D &=\Bigg| \dfrac{\% \text{change in quantity demanded}}{\% \text{change in price}} \Bigg| \\\\
&=\Bigg| \dfrac{\dfrac{\text{Change in quantity}}{\text{Initial quantity}}\times 100\% }{\dfrac{\text{Change in price}}{\text{Initial price}}\times 100\%}\Bigg|\end{align}$
The percentage change in quantity demanded is:

$\begin{align} \%\text{change in quantity demanded}&=\dfrac{\text{Difference in quantity}}{\text{Initial quantity}}\times 100\%\\\\
&=\dfrac{1000-800}{1000}\times 100\%\\\\
&=\dfrac{200}{1000} \times 100\%\\\\
&=0.2 \times 100\%\\\\
&=20\%\end{align}$

The percentage change in price is:

$\begin{align} \%\text{change in price}&=\dfrac{\text{Difference in price}}{\text{Initial price}}\times 100\%\\\\
&=\dfrac{\$1.00-\$0.50}{\$0.50}\times 100\%\\\\
&=\dfrac{\$0.50}{\$0.50} \times 100\%\\\\
&=1 \times 100\%\\\\
&=100\%\end{align}$
The price elasticity of demand in this price range is:


$\begin{align}E_p^D &=\Bigg| \dfrac{\% \text{change in quantity demanded}}{\% \text{change in price}} \Bigg| \\\\
&=\Bigg| \dfrac{20\%}{100\%} \Bigg|\\\\
&=\big|0.2\big|\\\\
&=0.2\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is the correct formula for price elasticity of demand?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: $\dfrac{\% \text{ change in Quantity demanded}}{\% \text{ change in Price}}$
$\%\text{ change in quantity demanded}\times \% \text{ change in price}$
$\dfrac{\%\text{ change in price}}{\%\text{ change in quantity demanded}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Three points along a linear demand curve are shown in this graph.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What statement about these points is true?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: The figure presents a graph of a demand curve in the first quadrant of the cartesian plane. The horizontal axis is labeled Q and the vertical axis is labeled P. The curve is labeled D and starts at the vertical axis above the origin near the top of the vertical axis and moves down and to the right ending near the horizontal axis. There are three points in the graph. The first point is on the demand curve approximately one-fourth of the way along the horizontal axis labeled 1. The second point is on the demand curve approximately one half of the way along the horizontal axis labeled 2. The third point is on the demand curve approximately three-fourths of the way along the horizontal axis labeled 3.

choices.content: Point 1 has the highest price elasticity of the three points.
Point 3 has the highest price elasticity.
Point 1 and 3 have elastic price elasticities, but point 2 is price inelastic.
All three points have the same value of elasticity since the slope of this curve is constant.
The price elasticity of demand cannot be compared for these points without more information.

assessment_items.question_content:
Three points along a linear demand curve are shown in this graph.


[[☃ image 1]]


**If the price elasticity of demand for point $2$ equals $1$, what must also be true?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Along a linear demand curve, the price elasticity of demand increases as price increases, and decreases as price decreases.

Values less than 1 are inelastic, values greater than one are elastic.
Points to the left of a point that is unit elastic are elastic and points to the right of a point that is unit elastic are inelastic.

options.alt: The figure presents a graph of a demand curve in the first quadrant of the cartesian plane. The horizontal axis is labeled Q and the vertical axis is labeled P. The curve is labeled D and starts at the vertical axis above the origin near the top of the vertical axis and moves down and to the right ending near the horizontal axis. There are three points in the graph. The first point is on the demand curve approximately one-fourth of the way along the horizontal axis labeled 1. The second point is on the demand curve approximately one half of the way along the horizontal axis labeled 2. The third point is on the demand curve approximately three-fourths of the way along the horizontal axis labeled 3.

choices.content: The value of price elasticity at point $3$ is inelastic
The value of price elasticity at point $1$ is less than $1$
Point $3$ also has a price elasticity of demand equal to $1$

assessment_items.question_content:
**What happens to price elasticity of demand as the price of a good increases along a linear demand curve?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Price elasticity of demand changes in value along a linear curve. Slope and price elasticity are not the same thing.
Price elasticity of demand is elastic at higher prices and inelastic at lower prices.
As price increases, values of price elasticity increase. As price decreases, values of price elasticity decreases.

choices.content: Price elasticity of demand stays the same.
Price elasticity of demand increases then decreases.
Price elasticity of demand decreases then increases.

assessment_items.question_content:
When the price of fuzzy blankets decreased by $10\%$, Mr. Fields bought $20\%$ more fuzzy blankets. 

**What is Mr. Fields’ price elasticity of demand for blankets?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When price elasticity is greater than $1$, it has an elastic demand. When price elasticity is less than $1$, it has an inelastic demand.
We can calculate Mr. Fields’ price elasticity of demand using this formula, and then examine the value to interpret that value:
Mr. Fields’ price elasticity of demand equals 2:

$\begin{align} E_P^D &=\Bigg| \dfrac{\% \text{change in quantity demanded}}{\%\text{ change in price}}\Bigg| \\\\
&=\Bigg| \dfrac{20\%}{-10\%}\Bigg| \\\\
&=\Bigg| -2 \Bigg|\\\\
&=2\end{align}$
A price elasticity greater than $1$ is elastic, so Mr. Fields’ price elasticity of demand of $2$ indicates he has an elastic demand for fuzzy blankets.

assessment_items.question_content:
**If the price elasticity of demand is unit elastic how does quantity demanded change in response to a $25\%$ increase in price?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Unit elasticity means price elasticity of demand equals $1$, which means for every $1\%$ change in price, quantity demanded changes $1\%$.
When price increases, quantity demanded decreases.
A $25\%$ increase in price decreases quantity demanded $25\%$ when price elasticity of demand equals 1.

assessment_items.question_content:
Farmer Brown bought $20$ cans of shark repellent when the price was $\$100$ and $15$ cans of shark repellant when the price was $\$150$. 

**Which of the following statements is true based on this information?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Goods have elastic demand when they are luxuries and inelastic demand when they are necessities.
We can calculate Farmer Brown’s Price elasticity of demand for shark repellant and use the value of that price elasticity to determine if he has an elastic demand or inelastic demand:
$\begin{align}E_p^D &=\Bigg| \dfrac{\% \text{change in quantity demanded}}{\% \text{change in price}} \Bigg| \\\\
&=\Bigg| \dfrac{\dfrac{\text{Change in quantity}}{\text{Initial quantity}}\times 100\% }{\dfrac{\text{Change in price}}{\text{Initial price}}\times 100\%}\Bigg|\end{align}$

Farmer Brown’s price elasticity of demand when price increases from $\$100$ to $\$150$ is:

$\begin{align}E_p^D &=\Bigg| \dfrac{\% \text{change in quantity demanded}}{\% \text{change in price}} \Bigg| \\\\
&=\Bigg| \dfrac{\dfrac{\text{Change in quantity}}{\text{Initial quantity}}\times 100\% }{\dfrac{\text{Change in price}}{\text{Initial price}}\times 100\%}\Bigg|\\\\
&=\Bigg| \dfrac{\dfrac{15-20}{20}\times 100\%}{\dfrac{\$150 - \$100}{\$100}\times 100\%} \Bigg|\\\\
&=\Bigg| \dfrac{\dfrac{-5}{20}\times 100\%}{\dfrac{\$50}{\$100}\times 100\%} \Bigg|\\\\
&=\Bigg| \dfrac{-0.25 \times 100\%}{0.5 \times 100\%}\Bigg| \\\\
&=\Bigg| \dfrac{-25\%}{50\%} \Bigg|\\\\
&=\Bigg| -0.5 \Bigg|\\\\
&=0.5
\end{align}$


Farmer Brown’s price elasticity of demand is less than $1$, so it is inelastic.

Farmer Brown’s has an inelastic demand, so he considers shark repellent a necessity.

choices.content: Farmer Brown has an inelastic demand for shark repellant, which means he considers it a necessity.
Farmer Brown has an elastic demand for shark repellant, which means he considers it a necessity.
Farmer Brown has an inelastic demand for shark repellant, which means he considers it a luxury.
Farmer Brown has an elastic demand for shark repellant, which means he considers it a luxury.
Farmer Brown has a perfectly inelastic demand for shark repellant, which means he considers it a necessity.

assessment_items.question_content:
The price elasticity of demand for a brand of breakfast cereal is $5$.

**Based on this elasticity, what will be the percentage change in the quantity of breakfast cereal bought as a result of a $5\%$ decrease in the cereal's price?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Price elasticity of demand is:

$PED = \dfrac{\%\Delta Q_D}{\%\Delta P}$

Which we can rearrange to find the percentage change in quantity demanded that will result from a change in price, given a good's price elasticity of demand:

 $\%\Delta Q_D = PED \times \%\Delta P$

In this case, 

 $\begin{align}\%\Delta Q_D &= -5 \times -5\%\\
\\
&=25\% \end{align}$
